slug: 你能不能同时爱两个人_关于多元关系
title: 你能不能同时爱两个人——关于多元关系

outline.concept: Love doesn't follow zero-sum logic — the 'pie fallacy.' Having a second child doesn't mean loving the first less; attachment capacity expands rather than divides. New romantic feelings work the same way — they don't automatically subtract from existing ones, though they do redistribute attention.
outline.query: 二胎 还爱老大 新朋友 旧朋友 爱被分走 养宠物

outline.concept: Different relationships satisfy different attachment dimensions. One person might offer emotional safety and routine, another novelty and intensity. Loving two people often means different configurations of intimacy needs being met — not two people competing for the same slot.
outline.query: 不同朋友 不同需求 倾诉对象 玩伴 安慰 冒险 互补

outline.concept: Jealousy isn't evidence that love has limits — it's a threat-detection signal about perceived resource scarcity (time, attention, specialness). Compersion — feeling joy at a partner's happiness with someone else — isn't innate; it emerges when security is high and threat perception is low.
outline.query: 嫉妒 占有欲 伴侣和别人开心 不安全感 compersion 吃醋

outline.concept: The real constraint on loving multiple people isn't emotional capacity — it's logistical. Time, energy, and the cognitive load of negotiation don't scale with feelings. Polyamory fails not because love runs out, but because coordination and boundary-maintenance require skills most people were never taught.
outline.query: 时间不够 安排冲突 沟通疲惫 边界 协调 多线操作 精力分配
